meta.title: React Performance Guide – Optimizing Renders & Virtualization (2026)
meta.description: Master React performance optimization. Learn how to diagnose re-renders, use React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, and implement windowing for large datasets.

content.value: React is fast, but unnecessary re-renders can slow down your application. A re-render happens when a component's state or props change. Performance optimization in React is primarily about ensuring that components only re-render when absolutely necessary.

By default, when a parent component re-renders, all its children re-render too. Wrapping a child component in `React.memo` tells React to skip rendering the component if its props haven't changed (using shallow comparison).

If you pass a function or an object as a prop to a memoized component, `React.memo` will fail because functions and objects are recreated on every render (new memory reference). Use `useCallback` for functions and `useMemo` for objects/arrays to keep references stable.

Rendering 10,000 DOM nodes at once will crash most browsers. 'Windowing' (or Virtualization) is a technique where you only render the items currently visible in the viewport. As the user scrolls, nodes are recycled.

value: **React-window**: A lightweight library for rendering large lists and tabular data.
**React-virtualized**: A more feature-heavy alternative for complex grid layouts.
**Performance Gain**: Reduces initial load time and memory usage by 90% in large lists.

content.value: Introduced in React 18, the `useTransition` hook allows you to mark state updates as 'non-urgent'. This keeps the UI responsive (e.g., typing in an input) while a heavy update (e.g., filtering a list) happens in the background.

rows: Large bundle size / slow initial load

faq.question: Should I use useMemo everywhere?
faq.answer: No. Memoization has its own cost (memory and comparison logic). Use it only for truly expensive calculations or to maintain referential integrity for memoized children.

faq.question: How do I find performance bottlenecks?
faq.answer: Use the 'Profiler' tab in React DevTools. It records why each component rendered and how long it took.

faq.question: What is 'Code Splitting'?
faq.answer: Using React.lazy and Suspense to load parts of your application only when they are needed, reducing the size of the initial JavaScript bundle.

quiz.question: Which tool helps identify which components are re-rendering unnecessarily?
What is the result of wrapping a component in React.memo?
options: It prevents the component from re-rendering if its props are the same
It automatically fetches data for the component
It converts the component to a class component
